entry.title: Unlocking OptML potential via user-defined functions for Business Uplift
entry.content:
Introduction

In many business areas the users need to find the right set of parameters or business tactics to ensure they can achieve their targets. This problem is resolved in many cases by trial and error by the very experienced users and relies on their ability to forecast or project how the changes they make have an impact on their bottom-lines. For a large part the problem of forecasting can be and is usually solved via Machine Learning trained on their historic data. However, in cases when you need to first provide the right (or "optimal") input to achieve your optimal target the problem is only partially solved. The business users still need to figure out the right set of inputs to provide to the machine learning model to get the desired forecast.

The technique described here has much broader applicability but if you are like me, it helps to see it applied for a concrete business problem. In this blog post we will address the problem by applying it to the real world problem that occurs in Trade Promotion Optimization for Consumer Goods industry. In this context we need to find the right price reduction tactics to ensure the optimal uplift in volumes and other derived targets like  net sales, profitability and ROI.

The focus of this blog post is the use of black box optimization techniques when the predicted uplift is defined by inferring from a pre-trained machine learning model. The machine learning model itself has been trained on customer specific data and understands the price-volume elasticity or in this case specifically the price reduction volume uplift elasticity.

The optimization model provides decision support to the business user(account managers in this chosen context) by exploring the space of different price points and temporary price reduction tactics like discount percentages vs absolute dollars off, Buy 1 Get 1,  Multi-Buy etc. All of these plus the communication  and display mechanisms have a significant impact on the projected uplifts even when the financial incentive value is the same to the end user. For example consumer behaviour is very different if they see a promotion of 2$ off on a product normally priced at 20$ off vs 10% off. Also the impact is influenced by whether the end user receives the communication of the promotion via a front-page ad(typically more expensive for the business but produces a much higher uplift) vs a coupon page or in-store communication when the consumer enters the outlet.

The ML infused optimization model, hence OptML, is deployed and served via components from SAP's Business Technology Platform to ensure performance and scalability requirements from the business and IT users at optimal TCO.

Optimization 

The business problem of deciding between the innumerable price reduction tactics can be solved via a mathematical optimization model. The choice of price reduction variables can be modelled by continuous, integer or binary variables. In addition there can be constraints on set of price points that are acceptable for example prices can only be $x.49 or $x.99. These influence whether the value variable can be continuous or restricted to integers.

Here is an example of set of such variables

 

 

 

 

Variables:​

   b : Binary variables if promotion should be chosen
   x : Continuous variables value if the promotion should be chosen
   y : Integer variables for how many units need to be bought by the consumer for the promotional incentive to be realized
  c : Binary variable for communication mechanism
  d : Binary variable for in-store display mechanism

 

 

 

 

The mathematical model then has constraints to ensure the variables behave in the desired way. Here is an a set of constraints to ensure the solution meets business needs.

 

 

 

 

Constraints:​

  Maximum number of offers
  Binding constraints between b and x and y​ # x and y can only take non-zero values if corresponding b variable is on i.e. 1 showing the promotion is chosen (and vice-versa)
  Maximum number of communication mechanisms
  Maximum number of display mechanism

 

 

 

Now we have modelled the variables and constraints that can model the business problem. However, if we use typical mathematical programming techniques like Linear or Mixed Integer Programming (LP or MIP) we have to describe the objective function as a linear term of the variables. The end user wants to find the variables which would give the maximum uplift or its derived targets. The uplift itself is known only via an ML model and is a function of the variables of the optimization model. We do not have an explicit linear model that can provide the uplift in terms of the variables. This is where Black-Box Optimization techniques come to the rescue, instead of defining the objective as a linear term we provide it to the model via User-defined functions.

User-defined Functions

We use the user-defined functionality provided by FICO Xpress.

The library provides functionality for expressing both the objective function and additional non-linear constraints via user-defined functions.

For our use-case the user defined function needs to provide the uplift or other derived target based on user selection as business users want to evaluate different strategies and tactics depending on the product, retailer, current market dynamics. Here is pseudo-code snippet showing the use of user-defined function

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

import xpress as xp
p = xp.problem("SAP BTP TPEO")

# Add Linear Variables and Constraints
#...

#Now add the user defined funtion variable and objective
target = xp.var(name = 'target')
p.addVariable(target)
p.addConstraint(target == xp.user(obj_func, b,x,y,c,d))
#...

def obj_func(b,x,y,c,d)
if target == 'POS'
   return uplift(b,x,y,c,d)
if target == 'GROSS_SALES'  #GES
   return cost_per_unit*uplift(b,x,y,c,d)
if target == 'NET_SALES'    #NES
   return net_sales(uplift(b,x,y,c,d))
if target == 'PROFITABILITY' #CP metrics
   net_sales = net_sales(uplift(b,x,y,c,d))
   investment_cost = investment_cost(b,x,y,c,d) #if cost is only a function of chosen promotion
   #or investment_cost = investment_cost(uplift(b,x,y,c,d)) #if cost is both a function of the uplift volume and chosen promotion
   return net_sales - cost
if target == 'ROI'
   return net_(sales - investment_cost)/investment_cost

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Optimal Solutions

The optimizer evaluates different options for promotional incentives and converges on the optimal solution. 

If the objective is purely uplift(incremental POS volume) or incremental Gross Sales(GES in the figure below) the optimizer chooses incentive which do not account for the additional investment required to achieve those targets.

An example optimizer solution development across the iterations and considering metrics like incremental uplift(POS), the optimal promotion is to give $3 off as discount.

However, now if you want to consider the investment needed and optimize for profitability the optimal solution might look for reduced discount that maximizes uplift as increasing discount increases the investment the manufacturer has to do by reducing its own margins and that of the retailer. The retailers would only agree to reduced price if they can still guarantee their minimum margins, which in term means the manufacturer has to give the retailer additional discount based on scanned units or bulk investments to ensure retailer still goes for the promotion.

For the above example now the optimal solution is to give only $0.50 off in discount, this reduces the uplift compared to before but ensures highest profit.

The optimizer can provide one single proposal which is optimized towards a particular target or a proposal weighted across multiple targets. It can also provide a selection of top performing proposals (promotional offers in this case) and allow the business user to select the best based on their current strategy. These proposal offers are used by the business users for benefit analysis.

Benefit Analysis

Ideally the account managers want to find options that are best across all targets but this is usually impossible as promotions with higher uplifts tend to be expensive and lower the ROI for the manufacturer and so the account managers find the best strategy for the product and retailer by analyzing the trade-offs of volumes vs profitability. The account manager needs to choose if they want to go for sales volume with first offer proposal(10 inthe figure) of Save $3 or drive profitability with second proposal of Price Reduction(12).

 

 

In some cases the solutions are easier for decision making for the account managers, for example for the proposals below the top 2 proposals with BOGO(Buy 1/Get 1 Free) and Price Reduction (with Feature Page) are competitive with BOGO being better for Gross Sales (GES) and Price Reduction is highest on Net Sales and Profitability metrics(CP)

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

With the optimized decision support system the account managers can now do the job they are really good. They can focus on the strategic decision making instead of trying out multiple options manually while still feeling they have not really explored the whole space of options out there.

Having a set of optimally selected proposals that account for the price elasticity seen from their historic data the account managers can do the next step of portfolio optimization (you guessed it, its yet another mathematical optimization problem, perhaps for another blog post). This helps them ensure overall profitability across all the products and retailers without exceeding their allocated budgets and providing their expertise and learnings from current market trends.

The account managers can take decisions grounded with learnings from historical promotions and optimized for their target while respecting the restrictions from their business partners(for example retailer margins), budget constraints, and consumer acceptable price points.

Architecture

To allow for dynamic scalability the following architecture was chosen for the above use-case. SAP's Business Technology Platform provides the UI, persistency on HANA Cloud,  and runtime via Kyma. As multiple business users need to run multiple scenarios for different products the system needs to dynamically scale up (and gracefully scale down after) during the promotional planning periods. This is enabled by scaling horizontally on Kyma as additional pods come up when the optimizer runs increase.

Conclusion

In many business areas the complete potential of data can only be unlocked by utilizing the learnings from predictive models to create prescriptive models which provide high-value decision support to the business users. This may seem as a challenging exercise at the outset but with the right set of ingredients this is achievable. The math and the technology are available to realize the benefits in a timely and cost efficient manner. This is achieved by empowering the experts with OptML decision support that combines Machine Learning and Mathematical Optimization together.

This blog post is based on recent customer work, for additional details feel free to contact the author.

entry.title: SAP and DataRobot Partnership Updates 2024
entry.content:
Since the announcement of last year’s partnership between SAP and DataRobot at Data Unleashed 2023, there have been some new changes and announcements around this partnership. In this blog post, we will cover the fundamentals on SAP Datasphere and the unique value of the partnership with DataRobot. Then, we will look at the updated Solution Architecture, the latest milestones of this partnership and where we stand today.

SAP Datasphere

SAP Datasphere makes up the core of SAP’s Unified Data & Analytics portfolio and enables users to create business data fabrics. Through simplifying data landscapes, harmonizing and delivering mission-critical data across systems while maintaining the data’s valuable context, customers can unleash the power of data to achieve faster and better decision-making.

In 2023 SAP announced its partnership with an open data ecosystem of leading technology vendors to provide a tailored integration between SAP Datasphere and their solutions. Through this, organizations can manage their data by making it easier to organize, secure, and control. Figure 1 shows SAP Datasphere’s core capabilities and a list of open data ecosystem partners.

Figure 1: SAP Datasphere and Open Data Partners

SAP Datasphere includes embedded machine learning and advanced analytics features that help customers condense their data into actionable insights for decision-making. Without needing to move data, customers can use SAP HANA Cloud’s data science capabilities to train ML models and store predictions directly within SAP Datasphere. The figure below illustrates how these machine learning and advanced analytics features are integrated into SAP Datasphere.

Figure 2: Embedded machine learning and advanced analytics in SAP Datasphere

In addition to the existing embedded capabilities, SAP has partnered with DataRobot to further accelerate insights and create AI solutions tailored to specific business challenges.

Partnership with DataRobot

SAP Datasphere and DataRobot partner together to offer customers a seamless way to leverage unified SAP business data fabrics for accelerated AI-driven application development. SAP Datasphere provides a unified business data platform, while DataRobot enables the creation of tailored AI solutions using that same business data fabric. This integration allows businesses to incorporate AI models built in Data Robot into SAP business processes and applications, delivering powerful, AI-driven insights. The collaboration bridges the gap between data science, analytics, and business users, driving more effective business outcomes.

Figure 3: SAP Datasphere and DataRobot Partnership

DataRobot is a comprehensive enterprise AI platform designed to build, operate and govern predictive and generative AI solutions, streamlining the ML process, and helping organizations extract value from their data. It offers end-to-end capabilities for both no-code and code-first users, allowing them to quickly create advanced AI models, facilitate smoother collaboration between teams, and monitor models in production. DataRobot supports diverse data types, including text, geospatial, and image data, and provides advanced algorithms for business challenges like classification, regression, clustering, anomaly detection, and time series forecasting. DataRobot provides a full life cycle development and management suite for generative AI solutions, allowing users to safely and efficiently build generative AI applications. Additionally, DataRobot was ranked number one in governance for predictive and generative AI by Gartner in 2024, offering explainability for AI features, end-to-end governance and monitoring, and best-in-class ML Ops and LLM Ops tools for production applications, helping businesses optimize their AI in real time.

Figure 4 depicts the updated Solution Architecture for using SAP Datasphere as a solution in SAP BTP together with DataRobot.

Figure 4: Updated Solution Architecture

The SAP and DataRobot partnership combines SAP’s business expertise with DataRobot’s AI-driven insights to improve organizational decision-making and operational efficiency. This collaboration supports a range of business use cases that can significantly enhance processes. Examples include revenue forecasting for better financial planning, predictive maintenance to anticipate equipment failures, demand forecasting using historical SAP sales data, customer churn prediction, fraud detection, supply chain optimization through accurate forecasting, and overall revenue optimization.

Roadmap Overview

As shown in Figure 4, the integration between SAP and DataRobot has significantly evolved since its announcement in March 2023. Initially, a JDBC connection was used to access SAP Datasphere data for modeling tasks, and AI model deployment to SAP AI Core required a Python script. Now, DataRobot can integrate with SAP BTP through various integration points and offers specialized connectors, including SAP HANA JDBC and SAP Datasphere connectors, improving read/write time, credential management and SAP Space management. Additionally, the new 1-click deployment to AI Core feature enables users to easily deploy AI models from DataRobot with a fully automated process, simplifying the deployment workflow and enabling full DataRobot ML Ops capabilities for models served in AI Core.

Figure 5: SAP and DataRobot Partnership RoadmapIn our next blog, we will demonstrate how businesses can integrate enterprise data with cutting-edge AI through the SAP and DataRobot partnership to create scalable value. In a step-by-step Walkthrough we will focus on how this collaboration addresses a key challenge for retail companies: accurately managing inventory to prevent stockouts. For large retailers, balancing scale with SKU-level precision is essential to ensure products are always available. The use case will illustrate how SAP Datasphere and DataRobot empower businesses to fully leverage their data, handling both scale and detail to solve complex challenges.

Acknowledgments

I would like to thank Farooq Azam from DataRobot for his valuable contributions to this blog. His expertise on the SAP and DataRobot integration was instrumental in shaping the content for this blog.

Further links

SAP Datasphere News September 2024
SAP News Announcement Open Data Partners 2023
SAP and DataRobot Partnership Announcement at Data Unleashed 2023
DataRobot
DataRobot Resource Library

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud sessions at Devtoberfest 2024
entry.content:
Hello SAP HANA Enthusiasts!

Devtoberfest 2024 has concluded, and we hope you were able to join some of the sessions. If you missed any, no worries! Here is an overview of the SAP HANA Cloud sessions for your convenience.

🔵 Embed the latest from SAP HANA’s AI functions into your SAP BTP Application with @ChristophMorgen 

In this session, Christoph introduced newly added Machine Learning and AI functions designed to enhance your SAP BTP Intelligent Data Applications.

The features include data-parallel AutoML (such as segmented AutoML TimeSeries), functions for vector data analysis, multi-label classification and regression, and more from the latest SAP HANA Cloud releases, as well as the Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) and the Machine Learning client for SAP HANA.

🔵 SAP HANA Cloud Multi-Model with @mfath 

In this session, Markus focused on the Multi-Model capabilities of SAP HANA Cloud, with a particular emphasis on the vector and graph engines. Discover how a robust and scalable data management platform accelerates AI projects through rapid data processing, real-time analytics, and advanced algorithms.

🔵 Advanced Strategies for Intelligent Data Applications on SAP HANA Cloud with @VolkerSaggau , @shraddhashetty  and @breglerj 

This session explored advanced features of HDI and HANA Tooling. Learn from Jonathan, Volker, and Shraddha about optimizing deployment with HDI parameters, utilizing the Fabric Virtual Table in HDI, and leveraging advanced HANA tooling features, including AI-generated SQL and CDS.

Are you new to Devtoberfest?

Check more about the event at https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest-blog-posts/devtoberfest-2024-welcome/ba-p/9358 by @thomas_jung 

Are you looking for sessions from previous editions?

Please check the Data && Analytics sessions at Devtoberfest 2023.

Enjoy your Devtoberfest 2024 experience!

 

--Vitaliy

entry.title: SAP CodeJam HANA ML in Wrocław 2024-10 Recap
entry.content:
At the end of October, we had the Getting Started with Machine Learning using SAP HANA and Python in Wrocław, Poland, kindly hosted by LeverX and organized by @Olena_Nadtochii. 

We had a fantastic group of participants, ranging from seasoned SAP veterans...

...to fresh talents who recently graduated from university.

This SAP CodeJam took place in a historical building—the former seat of the Faculty of Pharmacy of Wrocław University: a part of the university buildings' frontage, stretching along the Odra River in the northern part of the Old Town. The view from the top was breathtaking: from the Gothic Cathedral on the right to Concordia Design on the left—the Building of the Year 2021, according to ArchDaily.

And the food was equally fabulous.

I’d like to thank all the participants who joined me for this final SAP CodeJam of 2024!

The 2024 tour included 14 stops for me, and now it has come to an end.

Now, the next stops are in 2025, and I hope to see you in Spain ( @ajmaradiaga ), UAE ( @athavanraja ), India ( @Shilpa_Shankar ). Would you like to host such an SAP CodeJam in your location? Here are the details: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/quot-getting-started-with-machine-learning-usin...

entry.title: How to handle UUID in APL
entry.content:
As the adoption of UUIDs (Universally Unique Identifier) and GUIDs (Globally Unique Identifier) becomes prevalent, APL (Automated Predictive Library) users may face the case where their training dataset contains such IDs. These unique identifiers act as a primary key to differentiate each row in your invoices table for example, or your customers table. This blog shows how APL can work with input data containing a UUID. We will cover the two phases of machine learning: Train and Predict.

The UUID in SAP HANA

If you run the following SQL in SAP HANA:

 

 

select SYSUUID as "MY_UUID" from dummy

 

 

you obtain a big alpha-numeric value:

 

 

Now, create a view from it just to check the datatype:

 

 

create view MY_APL_VIEW as
select SYSUUID as "MY_UUID" from dummy;

select view_name, column_name, data_type_name
from view_columns where view_name = 'MY_APL_VIEW';

 

 

 

 

The UUID is stored as a VARBINARY, but this datatype is not supported in APL, therefore we will convert the UUID into a hexadecimal string. This conversion can be done with the function: bintohex().

 

Training dataset with a UUID

We need to train a classification model against this dataset:

 

 

 

 

 

Because the table contains a UUID, we must convert that column using the script below:

 

 

select 
 BINTOHEX("UUID") as "Id",
 "Days_to_Report",
 "Bodily_Injury", 
 "Property_Damage",
 "Age",
 "Gender",
 "Income",
 "Fraud" 
from 
 "APL_SAMPLES"."ACCIDENT_CLAIMS"

 

 

Make the conversion either via a SQL view if you are working with APL SQL, or via a dataframe if you are working with APL Python. An example of dataframe based on a select statement is shown here:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/multiclass-classification-with-apl-automated-predictive-library/ba-p/13545072

The SQL view for training the APL model looks like this:

 

 

 

 

 

Inference dataset with a UUID

The inference dataset requires the same conversion before you can run the APL predictions:

 

 

select 
 BINTOHEX("UUID") as "Id",
 "Days_to_Report",
 "Bodily_Injury", 
 "Property_Damage",
 "Age",
 "Gender",
 "Income"
from 
 "APL_SAMPLES"."ACCIDENT_NEW_CLAIMS"

 

 

The APL output from predicting claims fraud contains Id values that are in hexadecimal:

 

 

 

We must convert back the Id so that the APL output table can be joined to the input table:

 

 

select N.*, "gb_decision_Fraud" as "Prediction" 
from APPLY_OUT O, APL_SAMPLES.ACCIDENT_NEW_CLAIMS N
where HEXTOBIN(O."Id") = N."UUID"

 

 

 

To know more about APL

entry.title: The AI Adoption Journey with SAP Fieldglass
entry.content:
There has been a ton of excitement and talk about AI with SAP from existing functionality to newer developments on the horizons.

Personally, I've heard a lot about AI, but I have yet to bridge the gap fully in understanding what it means for what we do here at SAP!

I want to lock in and understand the changes with SAP software and its AI capabilities from the basics, starting with what AI is, practical use cases, and how SAP has used it to further innovation and development.

The term "AI" or Artificial Intelligence is quickly becoming a central focus in almost every area of development with technology available to us as consumers. The definition for AI is so vague that it covers so many possibilities. It becomes difficult to nail down exactly why it matters and how it can impact what we want to work on in the immediate future. 

The main purpose for AI is found in its capacity to perform tasks that, historically, only people could perform. These tasks generally involve anything that involves reasoning, decision-making, problem solving. 


AI sounds like its constantly in development, because it is! But its usage is far more accessible than it sounds, especially with a type of AI called "Machine Learning". 

Machine Learning sounds a bit intimidating, but it basically gives technology the ability to predict outcomes and classify information. 

In fact, you've likely already interacted with different forms of machine learning AI! If you have ever:

  • Come across recommendations based on your tastes in media (Spotify playlist recommendations, "For You" sections on any movie streaming services)
  • Used the speech to text feature on your cell phone, or any sort of transcription/translation features in teams
  • Gotten any alerts from your banking app about flagging or monitoring transaction activity

All of these things that we interact with daily, are all possible because of machine learning AI. 

As AI changes the way we experience technology, how can we leverage it to positively impact our business? More specifically, how can AI best benefit our SAP Fieldglass customers? 

Join me in a deep dive of the existing AI and Machine Learning features to understand the features and functionality and explore up-and-coming innovations with SAP AI in our new series The AI Adoption Journey!

entry.title: New Machine Learning features in SAP HANA 2.0 SPS 08
entry.content:
With the SAP HANA 2.0 SPS 08 release, several new features have been released in the SAP HANA Cloud Predictive Analysis Library (PAL), an enhancement summary is available in the What’s new document for Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) enhancements overview with SAP HANA Platform 2.0 SPS 08.

The feature highlights for the current release are described in more detail below

Classification and regression enhancements

A new multi-task multilayer perceptron (MLP) function is introduced, enabling multi-label classification or multi-target regression scenarios. Using a multi-task learning neural network, a single ML model allows to predict multiple, related target columns at once, as the model captures both common features across tasks as well as task-specific information with the same prediction model.

  • It leverages the commonalities between related tasks to improve the performance, generalization, and training efficiency, and moreover enables efficient use of data, better feature extraction, knowledge transfer, regularization, and end-to-end learning.
  • Furthermore, the function supports early stopping using validation data to avoid overfitting.
  • Users of the new multi-task MLP also benefit from shorter MLP training times and models with improved accuracy.

     The new function provides unique prediction model capabilities for example scenarios like

  • automated multi-field value proposals or pre-filling of forms (e.g. Sales Order Automation)
  • or predicting multiple price-/sales-targets (average, minimum, etc.) in a single model.

  • For a more detailed introduction to the new algorithm see the following blog post Advancing to Multi-task Multilayer Perceptron: a new Neural Network design in HANA Machine Learning  https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/advancing-to-multi-task-multilayer-perceptron-a-new-neural-network-design/ba-p/13730435

 

Unified regression and -classification now support permutation feature importance,

  • a new and trending method in global explainability to evaluate the contribution of individual features to the overall predictive power of a model.
  • This is achieved by measuring the decrease of a model’s performance when a feature‘s values are being shuffled around. A detailed explanation and examples are also given in this blog Global Explanation Capabilities in SAP HANA Machine Learning.

                  Classic feature importance vs permutation feature importance reports 

 

The unified regression function is enhanced with prediction- and confidence interval

  • Interval estimates and the point estimate of response values in the unified prediction or scoring regression procedure. The prediction interval predicts distribution of individual future observation of the target, while the confidence interval predicts the mean statistic of the future target population.
  • Now supported with Generalised Linear Models (GLM), Multiple Linear Regression (MLR), Random Decision Trees (RDT), and Hybrid Gradient Boosting Tree (HGBT).
  • Complementing this, a new interval quality function is added, which aids to evaluate the quality of an interval

 

A new outlier detection for regression function is added,

  • providing the ability to detect point outliers in data used when training linear or tree-based regression models using MLR and HGBT regressors.
  • Outliers in training data get identified based on residual analysis and outlier-score evaluation, then can be excluded for model training and allowing to build improved regression model predictions.

 

A new FairML classification and regression function is added

  • New function aiding to mitigate “bias” and potential “unfairness” in ML model predictions related to affected groups of individuals
  • Binary classification or regression models based on Hybrid Gradient Boosting Tree (HGBT) are supported
  • Fairness evaluation is aided with metrics like demographic parity, equalized odds, and more
  • For a detailed use case and function example see the following blog https://blogs.sap.com/2023/12/08/fairness-in-machine-learning-a-new-feature-in-sap-hana-pal/

 

The Hybrid Gradient Boosting Tree (HGBT) function has been improved

  • New objective function Huber Loss and reweighted square for more robust regression models
  • Improved classification models by support of F1, recall, precision as cross validation metrics and weight scaling of target values to address imbalanced classes or weight scale values related to different costs
  • For improved early stopping during model optimization, the validation metric for early stopping can now be explicitly set.

Further regression and classification enhancements included

  • Improved KNN search, by matrix-enabled search method
  • Option to limit results to top N classes in multi-class predictions with unified classification
  • Enhanced one-hot encoding of categorial features, supports aggregating infrequent values, into a single output for each feature (SVM, MLP, MLinR, MCLogR)
  • Data-parallel / massive execution support with Multiple Linear-, Exponential-, Bi-Variate Natural Logarithmic-, Bi-Variate Geometric-, Polynomial-Regression

 

Time series analysis and forecasting enhancements

New outlier detection function in time series analysis

  • Automatic or manual time series residual analysis, incl. intermittent time series detection, and missing value handling, auto smoothing of time series
  • Automatic selection of the outlier detection algorithm from the residual series incl. Z1, Z2, Inter-Quartile-Range, Mean absolute deviation, Isolation Forest and DBSCAN
  • Support of a voting ensemble across multiple outlier scoring metrics

 

Improved seasonality- and trend decomposition in time series analysis

  • Seasonal and trend decomposition using the STL-method (season, trend, low-pass window), based on LOESS (locally estimated scatterplot smoothing)
    and a super smoother function
  • Allowing for seasonality tests with more robust decomposition and less sensitive to outliers

Time series analysis permutation-based external feature importance evaluation

  • a new and trending method in global explainability to evaluate the contribution of individual features to the overall forecast model accuracy.
  • This is achieved by measuring the decrease of a model’s performance when a feature‘s values are being shuffled around. A detailed explanation and examples are also given in this blog Global Explanation Capabilities in SAP HANA Machine Learning.
  • Supported with the following time series algorithms ArimaX, Bayesian Structural Time Series (BSTS), Long term time series (LTSF), Additive model time series analysis (AMTSA)

Forecast prediction interval evaluation

  • a new interval quality function is added, which aids to evaluate the quality the forecasted prediction interval

Long term time series (LTSF) enhancements

  • New Neural Network model types (NLinear, DLinear, XLinear) for improved speed and forecast accuracy
  • Rigorous decomposition support of neural network time series models into TREND, SEASONAL and EXOGENOUS_CONTRIBUTIONS parts

 

New data-parallel, massive execution time series function support

  • Online BCPD, Forecast Accuracy Measure, Time series outlier detection, Single Exponential Smoothing, Double Exponential Smoothing, Triple Exponential Smoothing, Brown Exponential Smoothing, Croston's Method, Linear Regression with Damped Trend and Seasonal Adjust, White Noise Test, Seasonality Test, Trend Test, Change-Point Detection, Intermittent Time Series Forecast (ITSF)
  • Allowing segmented time series analysis and forecasting and fastest result when modelling thousands of time series in parallel, identified by a segmentation,  grouping column

 

 

AutoML and ML pipelines enhancements

General AutoML configuration and optimization improvements

  • Optimized pipeline operator selection, considering operator sequence and importance using operator connection constraints
  • Finetuning phase of best pipeline after initial AutoML optimization, by further evaluation the remaining parameter combination options and without changing the selected pipeline operators itself
  • Support of random search optimization for small AutoML configurations and faster results, for example with simple time series scenarios
  • Faster AutoML pipeline predictions interpretability based on a more light-weight shapley explainer model (SHAPGlobal surrogate). More details can be found in the following blog post Demystifying Pipeline Explanation for Time Series Data.
  • AutoML scenario configuration verification option
  • Pipeline predictions supporting more algorithm specific parameters
  • Score function for hold-out sample based classification / regression model evaluation
  • Improved AutoML progress logging in Python ML client, with full log information

AutoML Time Series enhancements

  • Support for auto exponential smoothing, polynomial feature generation,
    HGBT-/ MLinR- regression operators for time series
  • SPEC as a new evaluation metric supported

New data-parallel AutoML and ML Pipeline modeling functions allow for developing and executing multiple models in parallel by

  • building AutoML classification, regression or time series models in parallel on data subsets, identified by a grouping column,
  • using the new massive AutoML and Pipeline SQL procedures

This allows for fastest parallel AutoML modeling, like e.g. segmented AutoML time series. At the same time SAP HANA Cloud’s database workload management is best applied to control and limit compute resource consumed.

 

 

Further AI function enhancements

New Quantile Transform data preprocessing

  • The Quantile Transform automatically applies a non-linear transform, that maps numeric input columns of varying data distributions, into a uniform or normal target distribution making it more suitable as an input for many ML models.
  • Preserves the rank order of the data, and reduces the impact of outliers, makes data less sensitive to extreme values

New financial data analysis functions

  • Benford Analysis, trending algorithm used to detect anomalies in financial data
    • One of the (not so) well-known statistical observations is the fact that in many datasets the leading significant digits are not equally distributed. If all digits were represented equally, then they would appear 11.1 percent (1/9TH) of the time. However, when analyzing real-world datasets, it is revealed that the distribution of the leading digits follows the Bedford’s law, also known as the first-digit law.
    • It is now very easy to validate if a dataset obeys Bedford’s law or not. A first step means, very commonly applied in financial applications is to detect unexpected value distributions and e.g. potential fraudulent transaction data.
  • Hull-White Model, trending used in modeling the time evolution of interest rates, rates, which are required for price estimation of financial instruments based on interest rate derivatives.
    • To apply the Hull-White model it first needs to be adopted to match existing market conditions (interest rates). This is achieved  by providing the values of the drift term of the Hull-White model as a time series as input table. The simulation will then provide the mean value for a given number of simulation paths (also specified as an input parameter), their variance, as well as the upper and lower bounds.

New MLP Recommender function

  • Powerful neural network function to predict binary targets such as click-through rates, a fundamental part in recommender systems.
  • The MLP recommender function does not require specific user and item details, and can include extra data features, unrelated features without any user connection.
  • The algorithm employs a dual-stream MLP framework, capable of handling larger-scale and “complicated” input data, designed to boost the recommendation performance.

Text processing enhancements

  • New RDT model-based text classification
  • Improved TF-IDF providing control over stopword usage

Improved Clustering

  • Pre-calculated distance matrices supported with agglomerate hierarchical- and spectral clustering with the unified clustering function
  • Improved one-hot encoding support with spectral clustering

Data-parallel / massive execution support

  • Isolation Forest, Variance Test, Inter-Quartile Range, Fast Fourier Transform, K-Optimal Rule Discovery (KORD), K-Means, FP-Growth

 

Automated Predictive Library

Automated Predictive Library (APL) 2425 release enhancements

  • Reduced execution time around 15% for binary classification or regression models (gradient boosting), and around 35% for multi-class classification.
  • Better estimates and faster calculation of variable interactions
     See what's New 2311
  • Local explanations for time series See what's New 2403

 

Python machine learning client enhancements

The full list of new methods and enhancements within the hana_ml releases 2.17-2.22 are summarized in the changelog for hana-ml as part of the documentation.

Selected key enhancements include

AutoML and pipeline modeling improvements

  • Visual editor support for the AutoML scenario configuration

 

  • Massive, data parallel AutoML support 

  • PipelineProgressMonitor python runtime_platform specification for jupyter, vscode, bas, console
  • AutoML optimization enhancement with connection constraints and connection visualizations

     

More detailed references to the described enhancements can also be found in the New Machine Learning features in SAP HANA Cloud - SAP Community blog posts.

Furthermore you can find example notebooks illustrating the highlighted feature enhancements under hana-ml-samples/Python-API/pal/notebooks at main · SAP-samples/hana-ml-samples · GitHub, like for example H2SPS08_AutoML.ipynb, H2SPS08_RegressionClassification.ipynb, H2SPS08_TimeSeries.ipynb, H2SPS08_MiscEnhancements.ipynb and H2SPS08_Design-time code generation.ipynb.

Explore and enjoy all the new capabilities!
